acts_of_discrimination.other:
In addition to the items I checked above, my manager and, at times, coworkers created an overall hostile environment that felt targeted specifically at my generalized anxiety disorder and need for occasional work-from-home days. For example, when I first disclosed my diagnosis in June 2021 after a panic attack at my desk, my supervisor, Mark Jensen, repeatedly referred to me in team meetings as being "too sensitive" or "high strung" and joked that we needed to keep things "calm" so I wouldn’t have another "episode." These comments were always made in front of others and never directed at coworkers who also reported stress.

I was also regularly assigned late-shift and weekend customer queues with the angriest and most escalated customers, even after I provided a doctor’s note in August 2022 recommending that, to manage my anxiety, I avoid rotating closing shifts and constant high-conflict calls. Other Customer Support Specialist II employees with the same seniority level and performance metrics were allowed to stay on earlier, less intense shifts or rotate more fairly. When I asked why I was being given those assignments, my manager told me that since I was "already anxious," I might as well "get used to it" and that this was "good exposure" for me. This felt less like a legitimate business reason and more like he was intentionally ignoring my medical needs and punishing me for speaking up.

On at least three occasions (November 2022, April 2023, and January 2024), I requested to attend optional but recommended advanced de-escalation and leadership trainings that other Specialist II employees were encouraged to attend as a step toward promotion to Senior Specialist. My requests were either ignored or verbally denied with comments like, "Let’s get your attitude under control first" or "We don’t want to overload you, given your anxiety." However, coworkers hired after me, with similar or even lower performance numbers, were approved and attended those same trainings and were later promoted.

In terms of discipline, prior to disclosing my disability, my annual reviews from 2019 through early 2021 were consistently "meets expectations" or "exceeds expectations" with positive written comments. After I disclosed my anxiety disorder and requested schedule flexibility, I began receiving frequent written warnings for issues that other team members were only verbally corrected on, such as clocking in 3–5 minutes late from lunch or needing to step away from my desk briefly after a difficult call. One written warning from May 2023 explicitly referenced my "pattern of emotional reactions" as evidence that I was not a "good fit" for a customer-facing role, even though my call quality scores and customer satisfaction surveys remained above team average.

When my grandmother passed away in February 2024, I requested two days of bereavement leave, which is listed as a standard benefit in the employee handbook. My request was initially denied with the explanation that the team was "short-staffed" and that I had "already used up [my] goodwill" by previously asking for schedule accommodations related to my anxiety. I ultimately was allowed to take one day, but I was told I had to make up the hours by working an extra Saturday, which is not something I have seen required of other employees taking bereavement leave.

In the final months before I was terminated in October 2024, my manager placed me on a Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) that cited vague issues like "negative attitude" and "lack of resilience" without tying them to specific measurable performance metrics. At the same time, I was still handling a disproportionate share of escalated calls and late shifts. Two weeks before I was fired, I again asked HR for a more predictable schedule or at least a reduction in consecutive closing shifts as a reasonable accommodation. Instead of engaging in a real interactive process, HR simply reiterated that the business "needed coverage" and suggested that if the role was "too stressful" for me, I might want to "consider other opportunities." I was terminated on October 27, 2024, with the reason listed as "failure to meet performance expectations," despite the fact that my objective numbers (average handle time, customer satisfaction, and quality assurance scores) were in line with or better than some coworkers who were not disciplined or terminated.

Overall, the pattern of comments, denials of training and schedule adjustments, unequal distribution of difficult work, harsher discipline, and eventual termination all appear connected to my disclosed anxiety disorder and my attempts to request accommodations, rather than to any legitimate performance-based reason.
